text: The surgeon uses indocyanine green fluorescence to assist in taking down the infundibulum of the gallbladder, highlighting the anatomical structures during the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and isolate the cystic artery while coming down on top of the cystic duct.
The surgeon attempts to obtain the critical view of safety, focusing on the anatomical landmarks necessary for safe dissection during the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue using robotic instruments.
The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety, with both the cystic duct and artery visibly isolated.
The triangle of Calot is clearly visible in the surgical field, with the Ruvier sulcus observable in the background, providing anatomical orientation.
The surgeon approaches the cystic plate, with the Ruvier sulcus visible to the left of the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates along the narrow cystic plate, visually tracing its path upwards in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a double or triple pump technique with the cautery device on the isolated cystic artery, creating a visible thermal spread on the vessel.
The surgeon carefully preserves the posterior artery that travels upwards, applying a bipolar cautery device to control the area.
The surgeon controls bleeding at the cystic plate, with occasional back bleeding from the gallbladder visible.
The surgeon observes back bleeding from the gallbladder, with no bleeding visible on the liver side or cystic plate side.
